We Need to Talk About H&M and Greenwashing
==========================================

### Few things spark debate in sustainable fashion quite like fast fashion...and greenwashing.

[](https://substack.com/@thesustainablefashionforum)[](https://substack.com/@brittanysiierra)

[Sustainable Fashion Forum](https://substack.com/@thesustainablefashionforum) and [Brittany Sierra](https://substack.com/@brittanysiierra)

Apr 03, 2025

∙ Paid

13

4

Share

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Q32a!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1f1cfad9-99c6-4350-9c02-5fcc915e6035_4526x2548.png)

#### **Few things spark debate in sustainable fashion like fast fashion…and greenwashing.**

Both trigger outrage, defensiveness, and heated discussion, especially as more brands make bold sustainability claims and more watchdogs, regulators, and consumers demand transparency and accountability.

But what is greenwashing when there’s no industry-wide definition of “sustainable” to begin with? No universal benchmark. No agreed-upon baseline. No consistent standard for what it actually takes to be considered “sustainable.” How can we call something greenwashing when the rules of sustainability don’t even exist?

What does greenwashing mean when every brand, certifier, NGO, legislating body, and independent consultant gets to define sustainability for themselves, setting their own standards, their own metrics, and their own thresholds for what’s “good enough”?

That ambiguity makes it easy to accuse brands like H&M of greenwashing, and just as easy for them to argue they aren’t. Especially when the same third-party tools and certifications they rely on are under fire too.

“How can we call something greenwashing when the rules of sustainability don’t even exist?”

Who gets to decide what’s sustainable? And how do we hold brands accountable when no one agrees on the rules?

**Behind the paywall: The full breakdown of H&M’s sustainability report, what it gets right, what’s still raising red flags, and why the backlash online has been so intense. Plus: the Better Cotton controversy, the Higg MSI drama, greenwashing lawsuits, and the fragmented legal mess brands are navigating across the US, EU, and beyond.**

**If you’re working in sustainability, or trying to make responsible claims without getting burned, this is the clarity you’ve been waiting for.**

3 ways H&M is improving its footprint — and the hurdles that remain
===================================================================

Apparel industry experts weigh in on the Swedish fast-fashion giant's latest sustainability report. [Read More](https://trellis.net/article/how-hm-is-improving-its-footprint/)

 By [Elsa Wenzel](https://trellis.net/article/author/elsa-wenzel/)April 7, 2025(Updated on April 9, 2025)

An H&M storefront in Kiel, Germany.Source: Shutterstock / MP Photo

[](https://www.linkedin.com/sharing/share-offsite/?url=https://trellis.net/article/how-hm-is-improving-its-footprint/ "LinkedIn")[](https://x.com/share?url=https://trellis.net/article/how-hm-is-improving-its-footprint/&text=3%20ways%20H&M%20is%20improving%20its%20footprint%20%E2%80%94%20and%20the%20hurdles%20that%20remain&via=trellisgroup_ "X")[](https://www.facebook.com/sharer.php?u=https://trellis.net/article/how-hm-is-improving-its-footprint/ "Facebook")[](https://trellis.net/article/how-hm-is-improving-its-footprint/# "Website")

### Key takeaways:

*   In a fast-moving, high-volume industry, H&M offers a rare level of transparency about sustainability progress.
*   The company’s circularity efforts are growing but remain marginal.
*   Despite progress in reducing supplier emissions, the road ahead remains challenging.

H&M Group has increasingly positioned its brand, [for better or worse](https://www.thesustainablefashionforum.com/pages/hm-is-being-sued-for-misleading-sustainability-marketing-what-does-this-mean-for-the-future-of-greenwashing), around ambitions to reduce its burden on nature. The organization’s efforts include investing in circularity and driving down emissions across its thousands of suppliers and 4,000-plus retail stores. H&M’s [2024 sustainability report](https://hmgroup.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/HM-Group-Sustainability-progress-report-2024.pdf) revealed the tensions between its latest achievements and the work that remains.

In this era of hyper-fast fashion, H&M — with its quest for sustainability and relative transparency — provide a foil to upstarts such as Shein of Singapore, which ship polyester clothes at disposable prices straight to consumers and create [astounding amounts of waste](https://yaleclimateconnections.org/2024/09/shein-is-officially-the-biggest-polluter-in-fast-fashion-ai-is-making-things-worse/). Stockholm-based Hennes Mauritz, founded 1947, is one of few apparel giants that has shared a [detailed climate transition plan](https://hmgroup.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/Climate-Transition-Plan.pdf) detailing near-term steps toward its science-based, net zero target for 2040.

“We aim to use our power, scale and knowledge to push the fashion industry towards agreeing and acting on fashion being produced within planetary boundaries, to harm no-one in creating our goods, and to empower our customers,” the company stated in its latest sustainability report March 27. In those 35 pages, H&M explained how it has worked to decarbonize its supply chain, adopt “sustainably sourced” materials and scale circular business processes.

Here, according to veteran apparel industry sustainability experts, are three major insights from H&M’s 2024 report — with the relevant caveats:

1. Supplier emissions are dropping — but next steps look tricky
---------------------------------------------------------------

“What stood out was their long-term goal to shift to 100 percent renewable energy with phasing out on-site coal in the immediate future for Tiers 1, 2, and 3 including spinning to the finished project,” said New York-based Chana Rosenthal, founder and principal of reDesign Consulting. “Their efforts to decarbonize are proving beneficial by their reduction in emissions thus far.”

In 2024, H&M shaved down its Scope 3 CO 2 emissions by 24 percent compared with 2019 levels. Most reductions came from changing suppliers’ manufacturing practices and energy usage.

“H&M’s considerable investment in sustainability is laudable and their report is edifying,” said Ken Pucker, a senior business lecturer at Tufts University and advisory director at Berkshire Partners.

In two years, the company whittled down the number of coal fired boilers in its Tier 1 and 2 suppliers’ plants from 118 to 27. By 2026, it aims for zero. For example, among H&M’s efforts to encourage its mills to drop coal, it [invested in Rondo Energy’s thermal brick batteries](https://trellis.net/article/how-hm-will-push-suppliers-to-use-thermal-batteries-for-energy/) for suppliers’ plants.

For the first time, H&M was able to count the 12 Tier 3 operations using coal.

That said, the company grew Scope 3 emissions by 3 percent in 2024, due to a rise in overall material weight and a slight uptick in shipping by air.

_Credit: H&M 2024 sustainability report_

Indeed, H&M may struggle to reach its aspiration to reduce climate emissions overall by 56 percent by 2030, according to Pucker. That target would require 10 percent reductions of carbon each year and no revenue growth, he added.

At the same time, the company’s level of ambitions and disclosures lead those of most peers. For instance, alongside Patagonia, ASICS and Marks & Spencer, H&M is one of only four brands keeping emissions targets in line with United Nations goals of 55 percent reductions by 2030 over a 2018 baseline, [according to a Fashion Revolution 2024 report](https://issuu.com/fashionrevolution/docs/full_report_31_july?fr=xKAE9_zU1NQ). Behind Puma and Gucci, H&M scored among the top three brands on that “What Fuels Fashion” report.

In addition, H&M updates a [public spreadsheet of suppliers every month](https://hmgroup.com/sustainability/leading-the-change/transparency/supply-chain/), detailing more than 6,100 companies from Albania to Vietnam. Some 570 are Tier 1 product suppliers and the rest include fabric producers, tanneries and dyeing operations. Yet the brand doesn’t similarly identify Scope 3 suppliers, which include materials manufacturing, transportation and other activities.

In 2024, the company invested about $170 million in value chain decarbonization. Most of that went to using alternatives to virgin materials, as well as reducing fossil fuels and boosting energy efficiency.

2. Circular business is growing — but only to a point
-----------------------------------------------------

“Fashion is full of glossy promises,” sustainable fashion expert Anna Blom, of Stockholm, posted on LinkedIn. “This time, we’ve got numbers.” For example, resale and other circular business models doubled in 2024 to 0.6 percent of sales, over 0.3 percent in 2022.

However, experts agreed that this represents an incredibly small slice of overall revenues. Lauren Fay, a Seattle fashion consultant and founder of BFG Lab, suggested creating a new role, such as “a Chief Returns Officer, to help connect their product team with their returns data for better design.”

Less than 1 percent of H&M’s stores stock resale items, but that has tripled since 2022. In 2023, the brand teamed up [with resale platform ThredUp](https://www2.hm.com/en_us/sustainability-at-hm/secondhand/thred-up.html) to list secondhand items there.

The promised gains from circularity have not materialized, according to Pucker of Tufts.

Yet Blom found more to encourage. “The signs of decoupling growth from resource use are there,” she wrote. “Emissions rose by 3 percent, while material volume increased by 8 percent.”

In 2024, H&M disclosed that it created 524,739 metric tons of products. Although that’s up slightly from the 2023, it’s down from 561,087 metric tons in 2022.

The company spotlighted the use of consumer insights, artificial intelligence, digital product creation to optimize planning and balance production with potential demand, reducing waste across the supply chain.

3. Recycling ambitions are big — but so are synthetics
------------------------------------------------------

Toward its 2030 goal of using fully “recycled or sustainably sourced” materials in its styles, H&M reached 89 percent in 2024. Nearly 30 percent of that was recycled, toward a 2030 goal of 50 percent.

Helping that progress was sourcing more polyester from recycled sources. The brand used 94 percent recycled polyester in 2024, closing in on the 100 percent goal for 2025.

But Rosenthal pointed out that this count does not include component materials, such as linings, fill and pocketing. “By including all materials in the production of their product, it would perhaps improve their turnover for circular business models aiding in the product feasibility for recycling,” she said.

“H&M is pulling many levers to start moving towards circularity,” according to Tamera Manzanares, manager of water and company network communications for the nonprofit Ceres, based in Boston. She cited a collaboration with the Circular Design Consortium as an example.

“The potential impact of those efforts, however, will not be realized until the industry solves its core challenge of scaling post-consumer textile-to-textile recycling,” she said. “Notably, H&M is partnering with Infinited Fiber Company and Ambercycle to address that challenge, and Ceres would hope to see H&M and other fashion brands doubling down on efforts to scale systemic solutions for textile reclamation and recycling as a core activity to enable circularity.”

For the past year, H&M has backed a [new chemical recycling venture, Syre](https://trellis.net/article/why-hm-turning-away-polyester-recycled-bottles/) by committing to $600 million of eventual purchases of circular polyester. The startup is scheduled to open a plant in North Carolina this fall.

But the infrastructure for recycling industrial amounts of textiles isn’t up and running yet, noted BFG Lab founder Fay. Recyclers vying to [manage synthetic fiber blends](https://trellis.net/article/samsara-eco-uses-enzymes-ai-recycle-nylon-6-infinitely/) are still [relatively young](https://trellis.net/article/this-polyester-t-shirt-can-be-recycled-forever-if-french-startup-carbios-has-its-way/). “Also,” she asked, “with the threat that microfibers pose to our crops and our health, are we holistically considering whether producing mostly recycled fibers is a good idea?”

Although polyester accounts for 22 percent of H&M’s materials, it uses more natural fibers; 55 percent of its products use cotton, and H&M has taken steps to boost alternative sources of cotton, such as by investing in lab-grown cotton startup Galy.

Greenwashing: 20+ recent stand-out examples
===========================================

[Akepa](https://thesustainableagency.com/blog/author/akepa/ "Posts by Akepa") on January 12, 2026 

You may have noticed the world becoming more eco-friendly in recent years — or at least, so it would appear. But if you’ve ever picked up a product because the packaging had the word “natural” on it or looked recycled, [**you may have been a victim of greenwashing**](https://thesustainableagency.com/blog/greenwashing-in-marketing-and-how-to-spot-it/).

Greenwashing is the practice of making brands appear more sustainable than they really are. It may involve cynical marketing ploys, misguided PR stunts, or simply changing the packaging of an existing product while continuing to use unsustainable ingredients or practices. It’s a way for companies to appear like they care while also increasing their profit margins, as they’re fully aware that eco-conscious people are willing to part with more money for sustainable products.

You might remember the controversy back in 2019 when [**McDonald’s introduced paper straws**](https://www.independent.co.uk/voices/mcdonalds-plastic-straws-recycling-carbon-footprint-green-washing-waste-starbucks-a9041871.html) that turned out to be non-recyclable. Aside from the questionable practice of cutting down trees to make disposable straws, this was a classic example of a corporate giant pretending to address an issue — in this case, plastic pollution — without actually doing anything.

Another cynical greenwashing move is to slap a green label on something to make it appear more sustainable or healthy, as Coca-Cola did with **[Coca-Cola Life](https://www.beveragedaily.com/Article/2017/04/07/Coca-Cola-Life-axed-in-the-UK)**— that with 6.6% sugar was far from a healthy drink. You’d probably get less Life if you drank a lot of it.

Unfortunately, greenwashing seems to be a trend that’s here to stay. Companies are constantly coming up with new and brilliant ways to tell us lies. One of the best things people can do to protect themselves from these corporate cons is to get familiar with recent greenwashing examples. Awareness of the tricks they use can help you spot other talented greenwashers more easily, so let’s take a look at some greenwashing lowlights from the last couple of years.

#### _1. Innocent_

*   **Year:** 2022
*   **Who called it out:**[Plastic Rebellion](https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2022/feb/02/activists-accuse-drinks-firm-innocent-greenwashing-plastics-rebellion-advertising-tv-advert)
*   **Type of greenwashing:**insincere TV adverts
*   **Where:** UK

When greenwashing, companies project an eco-friendly image which is untrue. Sometimes the disguise is hard to detect but in other cases **the veneer strays so far from the truth that you begin to question the sobriety of the PR & Marketing teams behind the greenwash**. Did they really think _that_ would work?

Innocent Drinks is a prime example of the implausible approach. The company is owned by Coca Cola, [**the worst plastic polluter in the world**](https://www.breakfreefromplastic.org/2024/02/07/bffp-movement-unveils-2023-global-brand-audit-results/). The company also uses single-use plastic – which is terrible for the environment. Yet, someone, somewhere, thought it’d be a convincing idea to release twee TV cartoon adverts – featuring cute animals singing songs about recycling and fixing the planet. In an effort to connect the brand with these contradictory causes.

It’s greenwashing, pure and simple. It’d be funny if it weren’t for the dire consequences of all that plastic pollution.

Luckily, the ASA stepped in and [**banned the ludicrous ads**](https://www.bbc.com/news/business-60481080) after they were reported by Plastic Rebellion.

#### _2. Keurig, Lavazza_

*       *   **Year:** 2022-2025
    *   **Who called it out:**[The Competition Bureau](https://www.cbc.ca/news/business/keurig-fined-3-million-fine-1.6307150)&[ASA](https://www.asa.org.uk/rulings/lavazza-coffee-uk-ltd.html)

*   **Type of greenwashing:** misleading recycling & compostability claims
*   **Where:** Canada & UK

The global coffee capsule market is worth over $15billion and growing fast. But the mountain of discarded capsules remains difficult to recycle. The problem is you have to use specialist recycling services instead of local recycling bins.

As a case in point of how the recycling process can go badly wrong. **Keurig led Canadian buyers to believe they could recycle their single-use plastic coffee pods** by simply breaking open the top, emptying out the coffee, and throwing the empty carcass into recycling bins. Easy right?

Wrong. The capsules weren’t accepted in most Canadian provinces, except for Quebec and British Columbia. And even then the instructions didn’t go far enough. The City of Toronto had to revert 90 tonnes of plastic pods from recycling bins last year because of this kind of capsule carnage.

Keurig were fined $3million and ordered to change the misleading recycling claims on the packaging.

The compostability of capsules is also problematic. In 2025, the ASA in the UK banned ads from **Lavazza UK**, for claiming that their coffee pods were **compostable ‘eco caps’**. Technically, the pods could be composted but only in an industrial composting setting. The ads were banned because, taken in context as products for the home, they led people to believe that the capsules could be composted at home. But they cannot be composted in such a straightforward way.

It’s an important distinction because home composting and industrial composting are entirely different. At home, the process is delicate and slow – and only suits certain products. In an industrial setting, the special equipment is capable of composting a far wider range of materials.

**Pro tip:** at Akepa we find a coffee from **[our attentive local baristas](https://syra.coffee/)** is way, way better than a mediocre coffee from a pod!

#### _3. Nike, Lacoste, Superdry_

*   **Year:** 2025
*   **Who called it out:**[the ASA](https://www.asa.org.uk/rulings/nike-retail-bv-a25-1309100-nike-retail-bv.html)
*   **Type of greenwashing:** unsubstantiated ‘sustainable’ ad-copy
*   **Where:** UK

The next case is intriguing because it highlights the risks of using **‘sustainable’ to promote eco-friendliness,**which might sound innocuous compared to bolder claims like carbon neutral.

It isn’t anymore. The UK’s advertising standards authority landed three fashion retailers, Nike, Lacoste and Superdry, in hot water for **using terms related to ‘sustainability’ in their PPC ads**. In case you’ve no idea what PPC ads are, they’re the sponsored listings that appear at the top of the search results in Google and other search engines. One of the offending ads from Superdry is shown below.

Source: ASA

So what’s the problem here? Well, it’s a lack of evidence for **the sustainability of the products behind the ads**. In the words of the ASA: _“Claims must be based on the full life cycle of the advertised product, unless the ad stated otherwise”._

These rulings also stand out because they weren’t followed-up after a complaint from another organization, which is typically how it rolls. Rather, they were picked up by the ASA’s **active ad monitoring systems – as part of a fashion-focused investigation.**

For anyone working with online advertising, it’s an eye-opener. Not just because ads are being monitored autonomously but because terms like ‘sustainable’ should only be used if there’s strong substantiation and website backing.

The ads had to cease and it’s a warning against using ‘sustainability’ flippantly, without specifics. Some of the ad agencies involved must have had a rollicking.

#### _4. Windex_

*   **Year:** 2019-20
*   **Who called it out:**[Packaging 360](https://packaging360.in/news/sc-johnson-to-launch-windex-glass-cleaner-bottle), [Owen Rosenberg](https://www.truthinadvertising.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Rosenberg-v-SC-Johnson-and-Son-complaint.pdf)
*   **Type of greenwashing:** misleading plastic packaging claims
*   **Where:** USA

The glass cleaner by SC Johnson claimed its bottles were made from 100% “ocean plastic”. But before you start imagining intrepid teams of Windex employees out on boats wresting plastic from the sea, let me stop you right there — because that’s not what happened.

In fact, **the plastic used to make the bottles was never in the ocean.** It was pulled from plastic banks in Indonesia, the Philippines, and Haiti. This type of plastic is known as **ocean-bound** plastic because it would have otherwise ended up in the ocean.

But the problematic marketing doesn’t end there. Windex also claims to be non-toxic, but a lawsuit was filed against SC Johnson in 2020, claiming that **[Windex products contain ingredients](https://www.truthinadvertising.org/various-windex-products/)** that are harmful to people, animals, and the environment.

#### _5. H&M & Fast Fashion_

*   **Year:** 2021
*   **Who called it out:**[Changing Markets Foundation](https://changingmarkets.org/)
*   **Type of greenwashing:** insincere sustainable fashion claims
*   **Where:** EU

The fast fashion industry is notorious for its environmental impact, so a level of greenwashing is to be expected there. But you might be surprised at the sheer amount of greenwash that was revealed in a 2021 report from the Changing Markets Foundation.

They looked at clothing from major high-street fashion brands to check the truthfulness of their sustainability claims and found 60% of claims overall were misleading. That’s bad but **H&M were found to be the worst offenders with a shocking 96% of their claims not holding up.**

Put simply, virtually all of the Scandinavian Fashion Giant’s claims were designed to trick people concerned about their environmental impact into buying.

So, the next time you consider buying from a major retailer like H&M because of imprecise green-tinged language like ‘conscious’, reconsider. The percentages say we’re likely being lied to.

#### _6. Shein_

*   **Year:** 2025
*   **Who called it out:**[Italian Competition Authority](https://www.reuters.com/sustainability/boards-policy-regulation/italian-regulator-hits-shein-with-1-million-euro-greenwashing-fine-2025-08-04/)
*   **Type of greenwashing:** vague website claims and unsubstantiated long-term targets
*   **Where:** Italy / EU

The fashion sector’s responsible for around **10% of global emissions per year**, as well as a mountain of waste that unfairly burdens developing countries. The worst offenders are fast fashion brands, of course, and Shein, even more aggressive than most, is considered to be **‘ultra-fast fashion’**.

Nothing’s stopping such brands from improving but when profit comes so clearly before purpose, their environmental claims are always going to look odd. The greenwashing detector starts to hum.

So what exactly did Shein claim? Well, in the second EU regulatory action against the brand in little over a month, an Italian court hit the Dublin-based operator of Shein’s European website with a **€1 million fine** for “greenwashing”. The authority found that sustainability and social responsibility messaging – across its **#SHEINTHEKNOW**, **evoluSHEIN**, and **Social Responsibility** webpages – was **vague, generic, overly emphatic**, and in some cases misleading or omissive, including false or confusing claims about recyclability, circular system design, and the environmental credentials of its **“evoluSHEIN by design”** collection. The regulator also flagged Shein’s greenhouse gas reduction targets (25 % by 2030, net zero by 2050) as **generic and contradicted by actual emission increases** in 2023 and 2024. AGCM emphasized Shein’s heightened duty of care because it operates in a highly polluting sector with super-fast fashion practices.

Shein’s EvoluSHEIN range from a brand that’s faster than USHEIN Bolt

A good rule of thumb to make fashion more eco-friendly, from the mouth of Vivienne Westwood, is ‘**Buy less. Choose well. Make it last**‘.

#### _7. Hefty Recycling Bags_

*   **Year:** 2021
*   **Who called it out:**[Lisabeth Hanscom](https://www.truthinadvertising.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/05/Hanscom-v-Reynolds-Consumer-Products-complaint.pdf)
*   **Type of greenwashing:** false representation of the product
*   **Where:** USA

In May 2021, a class-action lawsuit was filed against Hefty Recycling Bags for the claim that its bags are **“designed to handle all types of recyclables”**. According to the lawsuit, not only are Hefty bags not recyclable but they also contaminate waste that would otherwise have been recyclable. This means many items held within Hefty’s bags, that could have been recycled, end up in landfills instead.

**Hefty bags are therefore no different from ordinary bin bags (and possibly even worse)**, and the lawsuit claims their misleading branding violates California’s anti-greenwashing laws.

#### _8. Ryanair_

*   **Year:** 2020
*   **Who called it out:**[Advertising Standards Authority (ASA)](https://news.sky.com/story/ryanair-adverts-banned-for-making-misleading-co2-emissions-claims-11926471)
*   **Type of greenwashing:** false low-emissions claims
*   **Where:** UK

Mid-flight ads, misleading luggage charges, uncomfortable seats. These are just a few of the reasons why people dislike flying Ryanair – and now you can add another complaint to the list: greenwashing.

In early 2020, **Ryanair audaciously announced itself to the British public as Europe’s “lowest emissions airline”**. The claim was more or less made up and the Advertising Standards Authority promptly banned the ads.

It’s a reminder that in environmentally sensitive sectors like aviation, you need to be extra careful as brands are all too eager to promote their green credentials to win more customers. Often the claims made just aren’t true.

#### _9. Luton Airport_

*   **Year:** 2024
*   **Who called it out:**[Adfree Cities](https://adfreecities.org.uk/),[Advertising Standards Authority (ASA)](https://www.marketing-beat.co.uk/2024/07/09/luton-airport-asa-greenwash/)
*   **Type of greenwashing:** airport expansion deception
*   **Where:** UK

_“If we miss our environmental limits, our expansion will be stopped in its tracks”_. That’s the straightforward yet incisive message that London’s Luton Airport ran in a series of billboard and magazine ads.

Source: [Adfree cities](https://adfreecities.org.uk/)

Sadly, as you may have guessed with our being on the topic of greenwashing, the message wasn’t to be trusted. The main reason being that **the airport failed to include emissions from flights in its environmental accounting**. That’s a colossal oversight when you consider an airport expansion would lead to far more flights, those flights would be a major source of emissions, and there would be environmental limits not to cross.

The complaint was first filed by members of campaign group Adfree Cities. The ASA upheld the complaint and banned the ads in June 2024.

Speaking about the case, Adfree Cities co-director Veronica Wignall said _“This level of deception is totally shocking – it’s like advertising cigarettes as healthy.”_

#### _10. Quorn Foods Thai Wonder Grains_

*   **Year:** 2020
*   **Who called it out:**[Advertising Standards Authority (ASA)](https://www.asa.org.uk/rulings/marlow-foods-ltd-g20-1061634-marlow-foods-ltd.html)
*   **Type of greenwashing:** misleading and unverifiable carbon footprint claims
*   **Where:** UK

Quorn Foods advertised their new product, the **[Thai Wonder Grains lunch pot](https://www.truthinadvertising.org/quorn-foods-thai-wonder-grains/)**, as a way to address climate change. The advert claimed it “helps us reduce our carbon footprint”, but what wasn’t clear was who exactly they meant by “us”.

It turns out they were referring to Quorn Foods, and not ordinary folk, who should avoid buying products that come in single-use plastic if they want to reduce their carbon footprint.

An inquiry by the Advertising Standards Authority ruled that the ad was misleading. It also noted that since the product in question was new,**it was impossible to determine whether it could reduce Quorn’s carbon footprint**.

#### _11. Shell_

*   **Year:** 2020
*   **Who called it out:**[The Internet](https://www.theguardian.com/business/2020/nov/03/shells-climate-poll-on-twitter-backfires-spectacularly)
*   **Type of greenwashing:** gaslighting of the general public
*   **Where:** Worldwide

Shell’s got some balls. The company is responsible for around 2% of global CO2 emissions from its activities every year, while it continues to plough ahead and invest billions in oil and gas. And yet, despite this, their marketing team thought it appropriate to ask their followers on Twitter what they were “willing to change to help reduce emissions”? Really?

The poll attracted a modest 199 responses but still went viral due to the wave of vitriol it received in response, including high-profile rejoinders from the likes of **[Alexandra Ocasio-Cortez](https://twitter.com/AOC/status/1323304992372129792?)** and [**Greta Thunberg**](https://twitter.com/GretaThunberg/status/1323355325798506504?).

This truly was one of greenwashing’s most egregious examples. But only a few months later, some karma was served when a **[European court ordered Shell to reduce its carbon emissions](https://www.rechtspraak.nl/Organisatie-en-contact/Organisatie/Rechtbanken/Rechtbank-Den-Haag/Nieuws/Paginas/Royal-Dutch-Shell-must-reduce-CO2-emissions.aspx)** by 45% by 2030 compared to 2019 levels. It’s the first time that a private company has been ordered to reduce its emissions by a fixed amount with a defined time frame (sadly, The Court of Appeal in The Hague overturned this ruling in November 2024).

If that wasn’t enough, here’s another case that took a whole year before reaching its end. Are we surprised that Shell has engaged in greenwashing more than once? No.

*   **Year:** 2023
*   **Who called it out:**[Adfree Cities](https://adfreecities.org.uk/2023/06/shell-adverts-banned-for-greenwashing/)
*   **Type of greenwashing:** misleading ads about clean energy business
*   **Where:** UK

In June 2022, the gas and oil giant launched a marketing campaign – featuring billboard posters, a YouTube ad, and a TV commercial – to promote their clean energy business in Bristol and the wider UK. The advertisements included misleadings claims about Shell’s involvement in providing clean energy and e-vehicle charging stations. In the physical billboards, they dared to say, “In the South West **78,000 homes use 100% renewable electricity from Shell Energy**.” In reality, it was coming from the country’s National Grid – a fact that was mentioned, albeit in a conveniently smaller font.

Campaign group Adfree Cities filed a complaint to the ASA, arguing the commercials failed to give any context on Shell’s total environmental impact. It’s true that the company does engage in clean energy but their overall business resulted in CO2 emissions of **[1.2 billion tonnes](https://www.reuters.com/business/energy/shell-emissions-dip-about-10-123-bln-tonnes-co2-equivalent-2023-03-09/)** last year alone. Meanwhile, spending on gas and oil production is still expected to increase. Somehow, their net-zero target is set for 2050. So the overall background isn’t all that green, is it?

The campaign was finally banned a year after its launch but doubts linger. Stricter regulation is needed to stop false messaging where fossil fuels are implicated.

What’s next, Shell?

#### _12. Unilever_

*   **Year:** 2022
*   **Who called it out:**[Advertising Standards Authority (ASA)](https://www.adweek.com/brand-marketing/unilever-hit-with-greenwashing-ad-ban-for-vague-environmental-claims/)
*   **Type of greenwashing:** unclear environmental claims
*   **Where:** UK

Unilever’s cleaning brand Persil is one of the UK’s most popular, with its washing-up liquid and dishwasher tablets used by **[millions of people each year](https://www.statista.com/statistics/1175738/persil-leading-products-in-the-uk/)**. Many of those buyers are becoming concerned about the environment, so it’s not surprising that Unilever has upped its efforts to appear eco-friendly. Nevertheless, Persil’s ‘Dirt is Good’ TV ads have been banned by the Advertising Standards Authority for unsubstantiated claims to be **‘kinder on the planet’**.

It’s the kind of perfunctory statement that’s not unusual from greenwashers, although in this case adorned by flimsy supporting scenes, such as a woman hashtagging **#plantmoretrees** on social media – and kids collecting plastic garbage from rivers and beaches. None of which explained why Persil was kinder on the planet.

The ASA took umbrage with the ‘various strands’ for being ‘unclear’. Kinder on the planet than what exactly? Meteorites, maybe. But all considered it’s fair to conclude that Persil, a typical laundry detergent, can’t be all that kind on the planet.

What to learn from this? It’s a case that shows if you are going make environmental claims these days then they need to be backed up. Someone tapping out an appropriate hashtag won’t do at all.

#### _13. HSBC_

*   **Year:** 2022
*   **Who called it out:**[Advertising Standards Authority (ASA)](https://www.theguardian.com/business/2022/oct/19/watchdog-bans-hsbc-ads-green-cop26-climate-crisis)
*   **Type of greenwashing:** misleading climate ads
*   **Where:** UK

According to lots of credible sources, the **[world’s biggest banks are funding the climate crisis](https://www.bankingonclimatechaos.org/bankingonclimatechaos2021/)**, mainly through investments with a significant impact on greenhouse gas emissions – especially fossil fuels.

HSBC ranks **[13th of the top banks financing fossil fuels in the UK](https://www.ethicalconsumer.org/money-finance/banks-climate-change-environmental-crisis)**. And still finances carbon-heavy industries like thermal coal mining. Yet, a wee bit of progress in greenwashing awareness was made when 45 people complained to the Advertising Standards Authority that HSBC’s advertisements were misleading. The offending ads were then banned but you can still see them in the image below.

One of HSBC’s climate ads

HSBC argued that they’re financing many of these industries to help them eventually transition to net zero, and it would be impractical to terminate funding. But while they highlighted their climate-friendly initiatives in their adverts, they failed to mention their considerable contribution to the climate crisis at the same time – an **[estimated $8.7 billion into new oil and gas in 2021](https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/business-60366054)**.

It’s an environmental nuance that was absent in the ads – and that, our friends, is greenwashing.

An irony of all this is that the day before the adverts were banned, HSBC were running workshops on how to spot and deal with greenwashing. They might need to take their own advice…

#### _14. Lloyds Banking Group_

*   **Year:** 2024
*   **Who called it out:**[Adfree Cities](https://adfreecities.org.uk/2024/12/lloyds-bank-ads-banned-for-greenwashing/)
*   **Type of greenwashing:** misleading LinkedIn ads
*   **Where:** UK

Now straight to a more contemporary banking case from 2024 which involved more or less the same story as the previous. The distinction being that instead of HSBC’s physical ads in places like bus stops, Lloyds Banking Group ran a series of **misleading social media ads on LinkedIn.**

The ad gave off inappropriate _vibes_ around the group’s eco credentials. You can behold the banned promotion below, where you’ll find plenty about what the company is doing but nothing about the contradictory incursions. For instance, the group may be putting ‘the weight’ of their finance into clean and renewable energy but that’s failing to mention the oodles of finance towards fossil fuels. In fact, $1.9billion in 2023 – according to Adfree Cities, who called-out the ad to the ASA.

Interestingly, the ad was crafted alongside a traditional marketing agency, adam&eveDDB, who seem to have got their client in hot water with insufficient knowledge of the pitfalls of greenwashing. It’s a reminder that a sustainability marketing agency like Akepa is the way to go for authentic sustainability ad campaigns.

#### _15. Delta Airlines_

*   **Year:** 2023
*   **Who called it out:**[consumer class action lawsuit](https://www.foley.com/en/insights/publications/2023/06/carbon-neutrality-suit-delta-airlines-greenwashing#:~:text=on%20May%2030%2C%202023%2C%20in,friendly%20representations%20false%20and%20misleading.)
*   **Type of greenwashing:**false carbon-neutrality claims
*   **Where:** USA

It’s not a sin to enjoy travelling, to want to discover new places and visit worldly wonders. But let’s be honest, it’s _extremely_ difficult, if not impossible, to make a trip carbon-neutral when flights are involved. Airplanes burning fossil fuels are one of the most prolific contributors to CO2 emissions. So while engineers and scientists search for alternative solutions, it’s a reality we should think twice about.

Which begs the question: exactly how delusional was Delta Air Lines when they thought it was okay to send this message in consumer-facing advertising?

“Travel confidently knowing that we will offset the carbon emitted in your Delta flight.” on a napkin. Source: **[Live and Lets Fly](https://liveandletsfly.com/carbon-emission-delta/)**

Back in 2020, [**Delta pledged $1 billion**](https://www.theguardian.com/business/2020/feb/14/delta-carbon-neutral-airline-plan) – for a plan including carbon offset credits and supposed reduced use of jet fuel – to cut the emissions created by its business. It’s generous, sure.

But now, California’s residents are trying to sue the company over their cocky statement, **“the world’s first carbon-neutral airline”**.

The case is proof of the power of marketing, showing how deceptive such a claim can be. Even when facts aren’t there to back it up. But more than that, it speaks to the long overdue need for greenwashing regulation.

#### _16. Active Super_

*   **Year:** 2023-2024
*   **Who called it out:**[Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC)](https://asic.gov.au/about-asic/news-centre/find-a-media-release/2023-releases/23-215mr-asic-commences-greenwashing-case-against-active-super/)
*   **Type of greenwashing:**misrepresentation of ESG-related investments
*   **Where:** Australia

What does it say when **[financial institutions are largely responsible](https://www.reuters.com/sustainability/banks-behind-70-jump-greenwashing-incidents-2023-report-2023-10-03/)** for the obscene jump in greenwashing scandals this year? When money (and profit) is on the table, the truth always seems to be a bit more open to interpretation.

Active Super, one of Australia’s biggest [**superannuation**](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/s/superannuation.asp) funds, has been linked to sustainability and responsible investments for years. It was even named Money Magazine’s ESG Super Fund Leader for 2022. This sparkling reputation had to be investigated after Australia’s Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) sued them for greenwashing.

Active Super’s sustainability claims have been called out for what they are (Source: [Principals](https://www.principals.co.nz/work/active-super/))

Active Super said they would stop investments that were harmful to the environment and community, including tobacco manufacturing, oil tar sands, and gambling. Russia, because of the invasion of Ukraine, was also supposed to be off-limits. Yet ASIC found evidence that says otherwise: holdings in the very type of businesses that aren’t people- or planet-friendly. And in Russian securities, too.

In June 2024, Active Super was found guilty by Australia’s Federal Court. At the time of making the claims, Active Super was holding stakes in a tobacco company, Russian oil and gas firms, and a coal mining company, among others.

#### _17. Anglian Water_

*   **Year:** 2023
*   **Who called it out:**[Advertising Standards Authority (ASA)](https://www.theguardian.com/business/2023/jun/13/anglian-water-ad-banned-pollution-record-sewage)
*   **Type of greenwashing:**concealing pollution record
*   **Where:** UK

In 2022, 10 UK water companies, including Anglian Water, repeatedly discharged sewage into rivers and seas – totalling an average of 824 filthy spills a day.

So it was cheeky of Anglian Water to then release a TV ad campaign, claiming **“everything we do today is for tomorrow”** and how the company cleans water by creating wildlife-friendly wetlands. Of this ‘everything’, their hefty sewage spills, which were certainly something, somehow got overlooked.

Anglian Water was fined £2.65 million after sewage overflowed into the North Sea.

And for that glaring omission, the ASA banned the ad. They did say that Anglian Water were carrying out activities that could have a positive impact on the environment. But they felt that Anglian Water’s message was contradictory, because the company also had a history of activities that were harmful to the environment (including fines) – and chose to omit this information.

Take it from us as a sustainable marketing agency: this is why it’s important that when you err environmentally, to admit your faults while moving forward. Don’t just shove it under the rug and hope nobody notices. Because they will. It can then taint the genuine action that you’re taking.

#### _18. Danish Crown_

*   **Year:** 2021-2024
*   **Who called it out:**[Vegetarian Society of Denmark](https://vegetarisk.dk/) and The Climate Movement
*   **Type of greenwashing:**misleading climate claim
*   **Where:** Denmark

In Denmark’s first lawsuit tackling greenwashing, Europe’s largest pork producer, Danish Crown, was taken to court and found guilty. In a victory for climate and animal welfare advocates, the Western High Court in Viborg asserted that Danish Crown misled its customers in its ‘Climate-Controlled Pork’ campaign, where it stated that **“Our pigs are more climate-friendly than you think”.**

“25% less climate footprint since 2005” claimed Danish Crown

Although the court found that the phrase “more climate-friendly than you think” wasn’t a fragrant violation, it ruled that the pink stickers claiming the pigs were “climate-controlled” violated Denmark’s marketing act as the label wasn’t subject to independent control. Danish Crown stopped using the label in 2021. But it wasn’t until 2024, following the court ruling, that the pork producer conceded it had greenwashed.

#### _19. Apple_

*   **Year:** 2025
*   **Who called it out:**[Deutsche Umwelthilfe](https://www.duh.de/presse/pressemitteilungen/pressemitteilung/erfolgreiche-klimaklage-der-deutschen-umwelthilfe-apple-darf-seine-apple-watch-nicht-mehr-wie-bishe/) (Environmental Action Germany)
*   **Type of greenwashing:**misleading ‘carbon neutral’ product claims
*   **Where:** EU / Germany

However you spin it, there’s no way that you can say that brands, products or services are ‘carbon neutral’ and result in no emissions at all. **It’s a step too far.**

That doesn’t stop brands from making such bold claims, though. But in a more educated – and more regulated – market they’re risking greenwashing bans by getting so loose.

To one of the planet’s biggest brands then, Apple, which has been ordered to stop claiming carbon neutrality for certain models of Apple Watch in Germany. The reason being that the claim wasn’t sufficiently backed, with flimsy support from (brace) Eucalyptus tree planting in Paraguay to offset emissions. These plantations have been criticised by ecologists who call them **‘green deserts’** but the court also found deeper issues. _“There is no secure future for the continuation of the forest project”_ , the court said. Three-quarters of the land in question was only leased to Apple through 2029, with no certainty of any continuity after that date.

_“This promise deceives consumers, because it is based on a carbon-indulgence scheme with an ineffective offsetting project,”_ remarked Juergen Resch, federal managing director for Deutsche Umwelthilfe (DUH), which brought the case against Apple.

In a way it’s a shame because there’s no need to take the extra steps and cross the line into greenwashing territory. The watches are still better for the environment than many similar products.**Precise information would do the trick**, rather than hubristic claims,

Apple is stil claiming carbon neutrality for the Apple Watches on its website in other regions – but we reckon not for that much longer…

Let’s hope that this misleading term starts to get.. neutred.

#### _20. TotalEnergies_

*   **Year:** 2024 & 2025
*   **Who called it out:**Fossil Free South Africa &[South Africa Advertising Regulatory Board](https://www.africanlawbusiness.com/news/21348-totalenergies-slapped-with-historic-greenwashing-ruling/)
*   **Type of greenwashing:**deceptive social media competition
*   **Where:** South Africa & France

In a historic first for South Africa, TotalEnergies – the world’s 19th biggest fossil fuel emitter – was found guilty of misleading advertising.

Greenwashing ads come in many mysterious forms and in this case, the deception emerged as **a hashtag competition on social media**. In the competition, TotalEnergies teamed up with South Africa National Parks (who really should have known better) to encourage park visitors to upload photos of themselves in the epic landscapes with the hashtag #FuelYourExperience – also tagging TotalEnergies and SANParks. The winners would receive a gift voucher and a weekend getaway.

Within the campaign, TotalEnergies insisted it was **“committed to sustainable development and environmental protection”.**

Yet, the evidence suggests more destruction than protection. Not only has the company increased its emissions but it also has a 62% stake in the Africa Crude Oil pipeline project, which will be the longest electrically heated crude oil pipeline in the world and could wreak environmental carnage along its path.

It’s a reminder that greenwashing ‘advertising’ doesn’t just mean straightforward print, TV or billboard ads. Other types of promotional campaigns like social media competitions count, too.

The decision follows South Africa passing its first ever Climate Act in July. TotalEnergies has appealed the ruling.

Then, in a later 2025 case brought by **[Greenpeace](https://www.greenpeace.fr/totalenergies-condamnee-greenwashing/)**, Total Energies ended up in court in France. In this incident, the company was **ordered to remove misleading website messages about aiming for carbon neutrality** (tsk!) or face fines of €10,000 per day. Just as well, as the company later stated it was going to _“ramp up production of gas”_.

This case marks the first successful greenwashing ruling in Europe against a major oil and gas company for spurious long-distance “net zero” claims.

#### _21. IKEA_

*   **Year:** 2020
*   **Who called it out:**[Earthsight](https://www.earthsight.org.uk/flatpackedforests-en#group-Summary-rayWKL9K1I)
*   **Type of greenwashing:** accredited illegal logging
*   **Where:** EU (Sweden) /Ukraine

IKEA is the largest consumer of wood in the world, and its timber consumption has doubled in the last decade. An investigation by Earthsight found that IKEA has been making beechwood chairs using illegally sourced wood from the forests of Ukraine’s Carpathian region, an area home to endangered beasts such as bears, lynxes, wolves, and bison.

Perhaps the most shocking thing about this case is that the **illegal timber was certified by the Forest Stewardship Council**. This oversight raises serious questions about the ethics and transparency of the FSC accreditation, which according to Earthsight, is not limited to Ukraine. They describe “rampant illegal logging” across the globe under the watchful eye of the FSC.

In spite of this, IKEA is among the best in the world in terms of its sustainability credentials, and FSC is considered the gold standard of forest accreditation.

* * *

With a bit of luck, greenwashing’s days will be numbered – especially with stricter new laws from the EU and other regions. People are getting tired of the same old corporate tricks and want to support brands that are truly sustainable. That’s why at Akepa, we’re committed to helping sustainable brands with their marketing so that consumers can make better, more informed, and more sustainable choices. **[Get in touch with us](https://thesustainableagency.com/contact-us/)** to learn more about our **[sustainable marketing agency](https://thesustainableagency.com/)**.

H&M used nearly 90% sustainable materials in 2024

 Despite this progress, Scope 3 emissions went up 3.5% year on year. 

[](https://www.csofutures.com/author/melodie/ "Melodie Michel")

Melodie Michel

Photo by M. Rennim on Unsplash

H&M is on track to meet a number of sustainable materials targets by 2025 and 2030, with 89% of the materials used in commercial products last year coming from sustainable sources – including 29.5% of recycled materials.

The Swedish fashion company made significant progress in the transition to sustainable sourcing in 2024. On top of maintaining the 100% ratio of cotton sourced from organic, regenerative or other sustainable suppliers, H&M procured 94% recycled polyester (up from 79% in 2023).

Title: H&M’s Greenwashing Controversy: How Ethical Fashion Brands Are Fighting Back

URL Source: https://www.opefir.agency/post/h-m-s-greenwashing-controversy-how-ethical-fashion-brands-are-fighting-back

Published Time: Fri, 13 Mar 2026 00:22:35 GMT

Markdown Content:
H&M’s Greenwashing Controversy: How Ethical Fashion Brands Are Fighting Back
===============

Helping brands push new boundaries

[](https://www.opefir.agency/post/h-m-s-greenwashing-controversy-how-ethical-fashion-brands-are-fighting-back#)

[](https://www.opefir.agency/)

[Home](https://www.opefir.agency/post/h-m-s-greenwashing-controversy-how-ethical-fashion-brands-are-fighting-back#)[About](https://www.opefir.agency/post/h-m-s-greenwashing-controversy-how-ethical-fashion-brands-are-fighting-back#)[Services](https://www.opefir.agency/post/h-m-s-greenwashing-controversy-how-ethical-fashion-brands-are-fighting-back#)[Clients](https://www.opefir.agency/post/h-m-s-greenwashing-controversy-how-ethical-fashion-brands-are-fighting-back#)[Blog](https://www.opefir.agency/blog)

[Contact us](https://www.opefir.agency/post/h-m-s-greenwashing-controversy-how-ethical-fashion-brands-are-fighting-back#)

H&M’s Greenwashing Controversy: How Ethical Fashion Brands Are Fighting Back
============================================================================

In today's world, consumers are more aware of the impact of their fashion choices on the environment. The demand for sustainable practices has led many companies to make bold claims about their eco-friendliness. However, not all of these claims hold up under scrutiny. This article explores the greenwashing controversy surrounding H&M and how ethical fashion brands are stepping up to challenge misleading practices in the industry.

### Key Takeaways

*   Greenwashing misleads consumers about the true sustainability of products.
*   H&M has faced multiple allegations and lawsuits regarding its sustainability claims.
*   Ethical fashion brands prioritize transparency and accountability in their practices.
*   Regulatory bodies are increasing scrutiny on greenwashing claims in the fashion industry.
*   Consumers can identify genuine ethical brands by researching their claims and certifications.

Understanding Greenwashing In Fashion
-------------------------------------

### Defining Greenwashing

Greenwashing is when companies make themselves look more environmentally friendly than they really are. It's like when a brand claims their clothes are made from recycled materials, but they don't provide any details about what percentage is recycled or how it's sourced. **This is a big problem in fashion because it misleads consumers who want to make eco-friendly choices.**

### The Impact of Greenwashing

Greenwashing can lead to a lot of confusion. Consumers might think they're making a difference by buying "green" products, but in reality, they might be supporting practices that aren't sustainable at all. This not only damages trust but also slows down real progress toward sustainability in the industry. Plus, it can hurt brands that are genuinely trying to do the right thing.

### Consumer Awareness and Greenwashing

People are starting to catch on to these tactics, though. More and more, shoppers are doing their homework before buying. They're checking labels, asking questions, and looking for certifications that prove a brand's eco-friendly claims are legit. Here's a simple checklist to help spot greenwashing:

*   Look for vague terms like "eco-friendly" without specifics.
*   Check if the brand provides clear details on how they achieve sustainability.
*   Be wary of big claims with no evidence to back them up.

> As awareness grows, consumers are becoming more savvy, pushing brands to be more transparent about their practices. This shift is crucial because it holds companies accountable and encourages them to truly commit to sustainable practices rather than just talking about them.

H&M's Role In The Greenwashing Debate
-------------------------------------

### Lawsuits and Allegations

H&M, the Swedish fashion giant, has found itself entangled in numerous controversies over its sustainability claims. **In 2021, a lawsuit filed by a New York resident accused H&M of misleading consumers** with false environmental profiles. The lawsuit argued that H&M's sustainability claims, particularly about its "Conscious Collection," were designed to attract eco-conscious customers while not truly reflecting the brand's fast-fashion practices. This legal battle is just one of many that highlights the growing scrutiny over fashion brands' environmental claims.

### Sustainability Claims Scrutinized

The spotlight on H&M intensified when the Changing Markets Foundation released a report accusing the brand of greenwashing. The report revealed that 60% of H&M’s sustainability claims were either unsubstantiated or misleading. This sparked broader criticism of the fashion industry’s tendency to make bold green claims without the backing of genuine sustainable practices. The Norwegian Consumer Agency also reprimanded H&M for using the Higg Index to support its sustainability narrative, questioning the validity of such claims.

### The Conscious Collection Controversy

H&M's "Conscious Collection" was meant to symbolize its commitment to sustainability. However, investigations revealed that this collection often contained more synthetic fibers than its regular lines, raising questions about its true environmental impact. In light of these findings, H&M has [withdrawn its sustainability initiative](https://www.retailgazette.co.uk/blog/2024/12/hm-sustainability/) and is reassessing its approach. This move reflects the pressure on brands to ensure their claims are transparent and authentic.

> As consumers become more aware and discerning, fashion brands are being held accountable for their sustainability narratives. The H&M case serves as a reminder that transparency and genuine commitment to eco-friendly practices are not just marketing strategies but essential elements for building trust.

The Response From Ethical Fashion Brands
----------------------------------------

### Transparency and Accountability

Ethical fashion brands are stepping up their game by being more open about their practices. They're publishing detailed reports on how they source materials and treat workers. Some even invite third-party audits to verify their claims. Unlike [greenwashing](https://thesustainableagency.com/blog/greenwashing-examples/), these brands are genuinely committed to showing what goes on behind the scenes.

### Innovative Sustainable Practices

These brands aren't just talking the talk; they're walking the walk by using innovative materials and processes. From biodegradable fabrics to zero-waste production methods, they're setting a new standard. Here's a quick look at some practices:

*   **Biodegradable Materials**: Using fabrics that break down naturally.
*   **Zero-Waste Production**: Designing patterns that minimize fabric waste.
*   **Recycled Fibers**: Turning old clothes into new ones.

### Building Consumer Trust

Earning consumer trust is no easy feat, especially when skepticism is high. Ethical brands are focusing on honesty and consistency. They engage with consumers through social media, answering questions and sharing stories about their journey. This approach not only builds trust but also creates a loyal customer base.

> Ethical fashion brands are not just reacting to the greenwashing crisis; they are paving the way for a more responsible industry. By prioritizing transparency and sustainability, they are showing that fashion can be both stylish and ethical.

Regulatory Actions Against Greenwashing
---------------------------------------

### Global Regulations Overview

In recent years, governments around the world have started to take a stand against greenwashing, particularly in the fashion industry. **Countries are realizing the importance of protecting consumers from misleading environmental claims.** The UK, for instance, has implemented the Green Claims Code to ensure that brands make accurate and substantiated sustainability claims. Meanwhile, in the EU, the Green Claims Directive is being developed to set minimum requirements for the substantiation and communication of environmental claims. This directive aims to protect both consumers and genuinely sustainable companies from false claims by requiring companies to use life cycle assessments and have their claims externally verified. [An overview of how different countries are tackling greenwashing regulations](https://www.whitecase.com/insight-alert/navigating-evolving-era-greenwashing-regulations-fashion-industry) provides guidance for navigating this complex landscape.

### Case Studies of Enforcement

A notable example of regulatory action is the investigation carried out by the UK Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) in 2022. The CMA scrutinized several fashion brands for potentially misleading green claims, as part of a broader effort to curb greenwashing. This investigation highlighted concerns about companies creating the impression that their products were sustainable without providing sufficient evidence. Such actions demonstrate the growing seriousness with which authorities are treating greenwashing allegations.

### Future of Fashion Regulations

Looking ahead, more stringent regulations are expected to come into effect. The Empowering Consumers in the Green Transition Directive, anticipated in 2024, aims to ban greenwashing and misleading product information. It will update the list of product characteristics that can be subject to misleading actions, including environmental or social characteristics and circularity aspects like durability and recyclability. As these regulations evolve, fashion brands will need to adapt quickly to avoid falling afoul of the law. This trend indicates a significant shift towards greater accountability and transparency in the fashion industry, paving the way for more ethical practices.

Consumer Strategies To Identify Ethical Brands
----------------------------------------------

### Researching Brand Claims

Today, the market is flooded with brands boasting about their eco-friendly initiatives. But how do you separate the genuine from the greenwashers? Start by diving into the brand's claims. **Check if they are practicing what they preach**. Are they transparent about their supply chain? Do they publish sustainability reports? Look for third-party audits or certifications that verify their claims. A brand that truly values sustainability will be open about its practices and willing to show evidence.

### Understanding Certifications

Navigating the world of certifications can feel like stepping into a maze. However, understanding these labels is crucial. Certifications like Fair Trade, GOTS, and B Corp can offer insights into a brand's ethical practices. Here's a simple breakdown:

These certifications can act as a guide to help you make informed decisions when shopping for ethical brands.

### Supporting Genuine Sustainable Brands

Once you've done your homework, it’s time to support those brands genuinely committed to sustainability. Here are a few steps to consider:

1.   **Buy Less, Choose Well**: Instead of buying more, focus on quality and longevity.
2.   **Share the Knowledge**: Talk about your favorite sustainable brands with friends and family.
3.   **Engage with Brands**: Follow them on social media, give feedback, and participate in their sustainable initiatives.

> By making informed choices, we can collectively push the fashion industry towards a more sustainable future. Remember, every purchase is a vote for the kind of world you want to live in.

For those looking to delve deeper into sustainable fashion, [discover sustainable fashion through estethica](https://estethicaglobal.com/blog/discover-estethica-your-guide-to-sustainable-fashion-choices) as it provides valuable insights into identifying eco-friendly clothing brands and embracing ethical fashion choices.

The Future of Ethical Fashion
-----------------------------

The world of fashion is changing fast, and sustainable fashion is leading the way. **Circular design, biodegradable fabrics, and on-demand production** are some of the [leading sustainable fashion trends](https://madefree.co/blogs/blog/the-future-of-sustainable-fashion-what-s-trending-in-2025?srsltid=AfmBOorahq8DH6SvvHvDZ5cEyejhn26NyulY4ZhYJyeIyV-xAWvWJV9G) shaping 2025. Circular design focuses on creating clothes that can be reused, recycled, or safely returned to the environment. Biodegradable fabrics break down naturally, reducing landfill waste. On-demand production helps cut down on overproduction by making clothes only when they're needed.

### The Role of Technology

Technology is playing a huge role in making fashion more sustainable. From AI-driven design tools that minimize waste to blockchain systems that ensure transparency in the supply chain, tech is changing how we make and buy clothes. Imagine apps that help you track the lifecycle of your clothes or virtual fitting rooms that reduce the need for returns. These innovations not only make fashion more planet-friendly but also offer a more personalized shopping experience.

### Collaborations Among Ethical Brands

Ethical brands are teaming up to make a bigger impact. By sharing resources and ideas, they're able to innovate faster and more effectively. These collaborations often result in collections that are not only stylish but also eco-friendly. It's a win-win for the planet and the consumer. Plus, when brands work together, they can reach more people and spread the message of sustainability further.

Final Thoughts on H&M and Greenwashing
--------------------------------------

In the end, H&M's greenwashing controversy highlights a bigger issue in the fashion world. As shoppers become more aware of what they buy, brands can’t just slap on a green label and call it a day. The backlash against misleading claims is real, and it’s pushing companies to be more honest about their practices. Other ethical brands are stepping up, showing that real sustainability is possible. They’re proving that it’s not just about looking good on the outside but also doing good on the inside. So, as consumers, we need to stay informed and demand transparency. Only then can we truly support brands that are making a difference.

Frequently Asked Questions
--------------------------

### What is greenwashing in fashion?

Greenwashing is when companies pretend to be environmentally friendly but don’t actually follow through with real eco-friendly practices.

### How does greenwashing affect consumers?

It confuses shoppers who want to buy sustainable products, making it hard to know which brands are truly ethical.

### What role does H&M play in greenwashing?

H&M has faced criticism for claiming to be sustainable while still using harmful practices, leading to lawsuits and negative attention.

### How are ethical fashion brands responding to greenwashing?

Many ethical brands focus on being transparent, using sustainable materials, and building trust with their customers.

### What regulations are in place to combat greenwashing?

Different countries are creating rules to stop misleading claims in advertising, especially in the fashion industry.

### How can consumers spot genuine ethical brands?

Shoppers should research brands, look for certifications, and support companies that are truly committed to sustainability.
